sentences.text: Auschwitz-- in the morning, they used to call you at 4 o'clock in the morning.
So-- so we used to go to the toilets.
You know, it was a big room, very large room, long one, with holes for the derrieres, you know?
And we used to go there to keep ourselves warm a little bit, so they-- the-- this is a block over there.
The-- the-- the ones, they were on charge.
They used to come with a stick to put inside the dirt, to-- to make you go out for the appell, you know?
Because we used to be outside for three, four hours standing there and shiver, you see-- no blankets, nothing.
The shoes-- no shoes, you see?
Sometimes we used to find, you know, little-- thing like Holland people they left.
We used to put, but we were not-- no, we had no shoes.
This is it, what it was in Auschwitz.
--there to go away, not to stand out-- inside there.
--with the only hole for the toilet.
There were only pumps, you know.
And then you used to wash yourself.
People, they didn't-- like I told you.
But myself, I used to wash with this, with water all the time.
Only when we were in the factory--
We were waiting to die only.
Because every time everybody was dying all the time, you know?
They say you can't come out from here-- only if the God is going to help you to come out.
And people dying, they used to carry.
And many time they used to put in a hole the-- one hole they used put all the people and bury them like this.
The Jewish people, the Jewish people share.
But we used to choose the people, like myself, my sister, Djoya.
My cousin, she was there, and another two friends.
So when we were passing through, we are still in Auschwitz.
And then we saw the trains there.
So before to go to the trains, they say you must take off your clothes.
And then they put us in a-- in a room.
We don't know where they were, all these showers, you know?
And we didn't know if it was gas or water to come.
And when we saw it was coming water, we start to be happy and crying, you know, because it was water-- no gas.
We knew already they were killing them with gas, you see?
They used to give a piece of bread so you go in-- just now have to wash yourself.
But when we went there, we saw.
We kiss each other, you know, even not sister or friends-- we ourselves, save.
And when we finish, they-- we put that dress-- no towels, nothing to dry, nothing.
So they put us in the train, you know?
And when-- we were so happy.
It was midnight-- midnight in the night.
Was the train far away from--
No it was the same trucks-- the trucks.
But at least they had some blankets there.
Truck-- truck-- no train, trucks for--
Yeah, where they put the animals.
And we were going, you know, like sometimes you peep, you see in the pictures, they go in.
Then you sit in the floor there.
We sit on this-- and shivering.
The cold was coming up to here-- burn in that so much, you know, from the cold, you know, and the legs.
And then we-- we went about two, three days to arrive to Wilischthal.
I don't know how many days.
So you stayed about how many months, you said?
All together we finished nearly one year we were liberated.
So it was about four-- five to six months.
About three or four days we arrived in Wilischthal.
And we saw that there were beds, you know, with mattresses.
At least we had a little one each.
So when we arrived there, we were happy.
And they said there is no crematorium there, because there were other people also working there.
And then they give us another dress, you know, to change.
And we start-- they say you must work in the morning, 6 o'clock till 6 o'clock in the night--
So I-- I used to-- I used to-- I was in the machine putting there the nails and close it.
Like say you're making a-- a ammunition, you know?
So you have to put a plug, and they used to make the-- I used to make with the machine, you see?
My sister, she used to control them-- if it goes in the thing, you know?
About-- still would-- our liberations, you know.
Yeah, we stayed there, you see?
But that-- in that where we went, in that factory, there were many Italians.
So they-- my friend, [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, you--
The sister, they were working with the Italian people, you see, inside.
And we used to know all the news.
The Germans, they leaving, you know, all these ways to have--
So in the nighttime, we had-- everybody had the-- the bed, you know?
So one day, she-- she pinch a piece of rapa.
The one you put in the soup--
She pinched one, and she put it under the bed, you see?
But when we used to go to work, they used to look if you've got knives or-- or salt.
Because the Italians, they-- they used to be-- they could walk, you see?
They were in prison, but they were not like the Jewish people, you see?
So they used to give to the girls salt or anything.
They said, who is this rapa?
And they see who is sleeping here.
Oh, she's going to catch her to put it in the soup, you know, the dishes for soup.
It was like barrels, you know?
And they want to put her.
They wanted to put her in the soup?
So she was running, running, running.
And the sister, she was crying.
And she said, oh, [NON-ENGLISH], don't cry.
And then we-- and then we left here ourselves what we used to hear for-- from her.
And then, after they didn't catch her, she says she's sick.

So in the hospital, what she used to do, they used to put in them the [? thermometer. ?] And because she was all right, she was not sick, so-- so-- so she used to take the thermometer and she used to do with her hand to make herself sick, not to come out from there-- because in case they kill her.
So like this, she save herself.
And then after, we were together.
And we met another Italian Jewish girl from Livorno.
She was an opera girl, you know.
She-- she used to all these songs.
And then we used to cry and sing it.
So one night, the [NON-ENGLISH], the blockova, you know, the German came up.
She was tall and blond-- said she is singing.
She gave her on in the mouth.
She broke three teeth from Frieda.
And she knew all the operas, you know-- opera singer.
And she lost all the teeth there, because the [NON-ENGLISH] came and beat her, you see?
And after, we used to go sing daytime, you know, and crying.
What work were you doing then?
I was work-- I was working in the factory.
We used to go-- come to the-- all the time, since they took us to go away from that factory, we used to work every time, everyday.
In nighttime we use to sit, but it was not bad like Auschwitz, because I told you we had a-- everybody had their little blankets, you know?
And we started to-- a-- a nice Jewish girl-- Polish, with her mother-- they were like sister and daughter.
They look like sister-- two sisters.
You know, the German, she was in love with her-- German woman.
They was making love with her-- the German with the Jewish girl in the block.
And we used to see it.
She used to bring their food to her-- you know, her food.
She used to put it in the little bags they used to have-- and these too.
So one night when I was working-- we used to work with the-- like I told you, with the Italian, with the Russian.
The Russian prisoner near, one day, he gave me his lunch, you know, to me.
And we had nothing here, you see?
Or there was just-- and I said to my sister, come.
Let us go to the toilet to warm and eat the thing there.
At least we were a little bit-- and another German-- he was Austrian-- Austrian.
He came and he say, ooh, that's good, good, the machine.
And he open-- he opened his bag where he had the lunch.
He gave me two little apples, you know, from his food, because the-- the Austrian people, they were very nice, you know?
So you alternated one week in the day--
The end of the war, these-- when they-- these Germans, they say you must come--
All the time in the camp.
All the time we were there.
We were about 14 of us, together.
I-- we knew we were working for the factory.
And do you know the German, they used to cut the hair to here?
And they use to have the blue jackets.
Because we never really thought to look.
I was with the-- you know, Esther [NON-ENGLISH]-- Esther [NON-ENGLISH].
And Mercardo Chadev-- I was together, you see?
Yeah, I suffered too much, and I cried too much.
And where I'm still crying, especially when I came out from the concentration.
They used to see me at night to tell the stories.
I used to cry all the time.
Even once I was in Rome, in the bicycle, and they see me.
I said, because I was there.
Because they were showing their arm, you know?
I can't believe it-- such a beautiful girl like you.
I said, yes, I was there, yeah.
Can you just tell me, during the time that you were in the second camp, which was called--
Is that-- what do you call?
To-- what do you-- 'Stelevi'-- in the factory.
They took us in the factory.
The camp, yeah, in the camp.
No, what is the name I told you there?
I used to work-- yeah-- no.
For one week in the morning, then--
At midnight they used to give us a little slice of bread, you know?
And-- and the-- the girls, the German they-- to keep up-- wake up, they used to put matches here to the eyes.
Because they were tired also-- the Germans ladies.
Yes, I remember they used to give, every Sunday, you know, they used to give us some potatoes with cottage cheese-- three potatoes-- with the skin and the bread, and the cottage cheese.
And we used to eat it, you know-- so happy it's Sunday-- every Sunday.
And then after, we used to take off the skin, we used to eat the skin, because we were hungry.
And then one day, one German Jewish, she knew that-- she ran away from the factory.
And for three days she was not there.
And when they were doing the appell, she was not there.
They say, if they don't find her, they are going to kill 10 people.
So all of a sudden, they were looking, looking, looking.
And she came back, because they caught her in this-- where she went.
They hit her so much in the head, in the body, the back black-- the head, they made it like [NON-ENGLISH], you know, all of these sores in there.
And all-- she was nearly dead in the-- in the floor.
And then they say, all the Jews, we must hit her, and we must put cold water on top of her in buckets.
And-- and we did this shame.
And I thought she's going to finish.
You know, after the war, when it was finished, and-- and we were there in the train coming home, we saw her alive.
And she was about 45 years old that lady.
But she was a German lady, Jewish, you see?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Where did you go from there?
From the-- when-- when you were in the camp in Wilischthal, where did you go from there?
They said, we must go to-- all the people from the factory-- to the train, you know, trains, like, again, the wagons, you know?
No, closing trains, closing-- only time closing, you see?
We went up there, and there they were going round and round.
And there they were throwing bombs.
We had only water-- for nine days, water and sugar.
No, only for water, you see?
And all of a sudden, we saw all the signs are written, Prague-- Prague.
We were passing all these villages, you know, and round, and round, and round.
All of a sudden, the train stopped, and they left us there.
And all the Germans, they used to be with the clothes, soldiers' clothes.
They change themselves civilian, and they run away.
All of a sudden, we see people coming.
They were-- said, don't be afraid.
We-- they took us to Theresienstadt.
And then they-- to give a nice bath, you know, put [? fluid ?] here, all over.
Because if you sick, you are sick.
In any case, they took care of us.
They give us a nice clothes, nightie, a coat, and [NON-ENGLISH], all this.
And then I said to my sister, I'm going to go around again.
The-- the-- in Theresienstadt, the-- the Red Cross.
He said that time they put-- also in there-- they-- they had also crematorium there, ready to-- to kill all these Jewish that were there.
When we were there, they-- they took us to Theresienstadt.
On the 8th of May, it's finished, the war.
How did you know that it was finished, the war?
You know, they were talking, the other people.
And-- and the German-- the Jewish people, they start taking the Germans like prisoners-- you know, the Jewish people, the ones that were in Theresienstadt.
So they give us nice food, nice soup, for two weeks.
And then when you wanted to go anywhere, you used to show the arm with the number.
You didn't pay no water, no cafe, nothing, you see?
They used to give you for nothing, these people.
And after two weeks, we were in Theresienstadt there, two, three weeks, I was walking, and I saw my cousin, Clara.
She was in the typhus verboten.
After a few weeks, she was better.
She came with us, with that-- where we were, the Jewish people, like Matilda, Israel.
All these people, they were together.
In Theresienstadt, it was a very big camp.
But they gave us nice food.
The Red Cross, they give us parcels.
As soon as we start eating, our period came.
And we used to make food, fete, you know, dancing and singing, you know, from the joys, you know?
Yeah, they used to put-- yeah, they put [NON-ENGLISH], they used to call it.
They told me, the people there-- the French, that lady.
She said they put things, no, not to come that thing, you know?
In the food they use to put, but we didn't know nothing, you see?
Just went there maybe we were malnoutritions.
We had no food, that's why.
Myself, I haven't got very much, on-- very fine hair, you know?
And rheumatism terrible, you know, from the--
A lot-- my legs, my foot, and my arms, you know, from buckets.
And we were-- and we never saw the snow in Rhodos.
--it was Jewish-- the-- all the Jewish people there.
You know, it was very nice.
No, no, no, no working-- sitting, eating all day, you know?
They throw away cigarettes to us, chocolates.
And then what we did is-- [NON-ENGLISH], you know, Rochelle, she was married to Flor's brother, Kapuluto.
So now-- she said now is the time we are going to go and-- and-- and pinch things.
So we pinch all these pillow cases.
And it was all plumas, what do you call?
We used to-- and then from that material, we used to make shirts for us, you know, to change ourselves.
And it was very nice, you know?
And then we saw all the Germans running like the Jewish people.
And we were-- many people, they throw stones to the German.
I didn't throw them, you know?
Do you have a hatred toward the Germans now?
No, because you know, there it was the war, you know?
Many Germans are nice-- the one-- the-- the ones that are not SS, you see?
Here there is two, three people here they live.
They are very nice, you know?
I feel that the Germans, you know, destroy ours family, and you know, all these people kill.
But you can't do nothing when the war, you know.
We came back, three-- Djoya, myself, and Mauricio, who died also.
Because he was there, he died young.
Yeah, he got married, then he got two children.
We were liberated there at that time.
And we stayed three two, three weeks, you see?
And then they start-- they start coming to take us home.
Say, where do you want to go?
We were happy to go home, to Italy.
To Rome, you see, because I had my sister there.
So we arrived in Milan for 10 days, you see?
Like that I said, from Auschwitz.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] And we were going-- ah, you left the telephone.
They took-- they put them on the train, the people.
The-- the-- the people they say, you are Italian.
You must go over there in Italy.
So they put us in the train.
So when we went-- we were in Vienna.
The-- the-- the Russians came, and we changed the-- the trains.
We're-- we take about 12 with us.
So they went to Paris and things.
We went to Milan after the liberation, you see?
And the doctors look at after you, see if you've got cough, if you've got TB, all the things they look out-- the Italians.
Who-- who received you in Milan?
The doctors there, when we arrived there, the-- the survivors.
So they came there, and the-- yeah.
In Milan we stay about 10 days.
And I say I want to look to Rome, because I've got a sister there.
And I didn't know there there isn't things.
She survived with the four children.
So she was in Rome, and we went there to live with there.
